track_name: Cloud Engineer / DevOps Engineer / Cyber Security Engineer

semesters.focus: Programming Foundations + DSA Basics + Git + Web Introduction
semesters.objective: By the end of II‑I, every student can write basic programs in Python/Java/C, understand fundamental DSA, use Git/GitHub, and have a taste of HTML/CSS/JS.

topics.text: Syntax, variables, conditionals, loops, functions, I/O.
Basic OOP: classes, objects, methods, constructors.
Time and space complexity intuition (Big‑O at a high level).
Simple problems from platforms (2 h of guided practice).
Git basics: init, add, commit, log, branch, merge.
Remote repositories: clone, push, pull, pull request workflow.
Classroom activity: students push a small programming assignment repo.
HTML fundamentals: structure, forms, common tags.
CSS essentials: selectors, box model, basic layout.
JavaScript intro: variables, functions, DOM basics, events.
SDLC models; emphasis on Agile/Scrum basics (roles, ceremonies, user stories).
Versioning + issue tracking (GitHub Issues, basic Kanban). Deliverables by end of II‑I:
A GitHub profile with at least 2 small coding repos and 1 static portfolio page.

semesters.post_assessment: Baseline coding test to decide who leans to SE/FSD vs Data vs Cloud/DevOps/Security.

semesters.focus: Strengthen DSA + Teach SQL + Start Track‑oriented modules
semesters.split_info: Common Modules - 24 h + Track Specific Modules - 24 h

details.text: Data structures: arrays, linked lists, stacks, queues, hash maps, basic trees.
Algorithms: searching, sorting, introduction to recursion and greedy ideas.
4–6 h of problem‑solving labs (LeetCode/GFG‑style curated sets).
ER modeling basics.
SQL: CREATE, SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, WHERE, JOINS, GROUP BY, HAVING.
Simple lab: design and query a mini student‑course database.

details.title: Shell & File System
Linux shell usage (bash), directory structure, navigation.
Working with files and directories (create, move, delete, view).
Permissions & Ownership
Users, groups, file permissions (r/w/x).
Changing permissions and ownership (chmod, chown, chgrp).
Basic Process Management
Viewing processes (ps, top, htop), killing processes.
Basic service management (start/stop/status where allowed). AI-awareness hook (conceptual, 10–15 mins inside this block):

details.text: Explanation on how logs from Linux systems (auth logs, syslogs, process logs) become input data for AI/ML-based security monitoring – e.g., anomaly detection on login behavior or unusual process activity. Expected Outcome: Students comfortably use Linux and understand that system activity and logs they work with are later used by AI-based SOC tools and SIEMs for detection.

details.title: Core Networking Concepts
IP addressing (IPv4 basics), ports and services.
DNS concept, how domain names resolve to IPs.
TCP vs UDP behavior, common application protocols (HTTP/HTTPS, DNS, SSH).
Security-relevant Networking
High-level view of firewalls, basic packet flow.
What it means for ports to be open/closed/filtered, relation to attack surface. AI-awareness hook (20–30 mins inside this block):

details.text: Discussion at a conceptual level on how network flows (source IP, destination IP, ports, protocol, packet counts/bytes) are used as features for ML models that perform:
Intrusion Detection / Intrusion Prevention (IDS/IPS).
Anomaly detection (identifying unusual traffic patterns).

Example: “If you captured NetFlow data from routers, those fields become columns in a dataset used by an ML model to classify Benign vs Attack.” Expected Outcome: Students understand networking both as a connectivity concept and as a data source for AI‑driven threat detection.

details.title: What is Cloud Computing?
Characteristics: on-demand, scalable, pay-as-you-go.
Service models: IaaS, PaaS, SaaS.
Core Cloud Services (across AWS/Azure/GCP)
Compute: EC2/VMs or their equivalents.
Storage: S3/Blob storage basics.
Databases: RDS/managed DB concept.
IAM (Identity and Access Management) foundational ideas: users, roles, policies.
Cloud Security Basics
Shared responsibility model (what cloud secures vs what customer secures).
Simple examples of misconfigurations (public S3 bucket, weak IAM policies). AI-awareness hook (20–30 mins inside this block):

details.text: Introduction on AI in cloud security, at a conceptual level:
Cloud providers offer AI-based security services (e.g., anomaly detection in cloud logs, intelligent threat detection in accounts).
Explanation on cloud logs (CloudTrail, activity logs, access logs) are fed into AI/ML services for:
Detecting unusual login/access behavior.
Spotting misconfigurations and suspicious activities automatically.

Briefly mentioning that later (III year), the students will see how such AI-driven security ideas can be prototyped using ML libraries on log/traffic datasets. Expected Outcome: Students see cloud not only as infrastructure but also as a platform that provides AI-powered security monitoring and anomaly detection services.

pre_requisites: (Linux Basics - 10 h + Networking Fundamentals - 8 h + Cloud Concepts Overview - 6 h) Pre-requisites: Students should be comfortable with basic computer usage and OS concepts, simple command‑line work (any OS), and introductory networking ideas (what is an IP address, what is a website/server), plus have very basic programming literacy (at least one language at “hello‑world, variables, loops” level) so that this module can focus on hands‑on Linux shell usage, foundational networking, and cloud/security concepts with AI‑aware examples.

semesters.semester_title: Deep Tech - Cloud / DevOps / Security Track
semesters.description: (Cloud Engineer Foundation - 18 h + Linux & Scripting – 10 h + DevOps Essentials – 10 h + Security Fundamentals – 10 h)
pre_requisites: Students should be comfortable with basic programming (any language, at
“variables–loops–functions” level), general OS/computer use and simple command‑line work,
introductory networking ideas (IP, ports, what a server is), and have seen at least one basic
web/DB‑backed app or cloud overview, so this semester can focus on hands‑on cloud resource
management, Linux scripting, DevOps pipelines, and security basics with AI‑aware monitoring

details.title: Choose one primary cloud (AWS or Azure or GCP) for labs.
Compute: EC2/VMs – provisioning, basic configurations.
Storage: S3/Blob – buckets/containers, permissions, basic lifecycle.
Networking: VPC/VNet – subnets, security groups/NSGs, basic routing.
Hands-on lab: deploy a simple VM + attach storage + secure with security group rules.
IAM concepts: users, roles, groups, policies.
Principle of least privilege, importance for Cloud/DevSecOps.
Common misconfigurations (over-permissive roles, public buckets, exposed keys).
Lab: create a role/user with limited permissions and test access.

topics.title: AI in Cloud Security – Conceptual Session (4 h)

details.title: How cloud providers embed AI/ML into security services:

details.text: Examples: AI-driven anomaly detection on CloudTrail / activity logs, login patterns, and API usage.

details.title: Concept of AI-based Intrusion Prevention/Detection Systems (AI‑IPS/AI‑IDS) in cloud:

details.text: Analyzing traffic across VPC/VNet for unusual patterns.

details.title: Mapping earlier knowledge:

details.text: Show that metrics students see (IP, ports, bytes, requests, log entries) become features for ML models in real tools (e.g., AI‑IPS, AIOps tools analysing cloud metrics).

details.title: Short case discussion:

details.text: Example scenario where an AI-based service flags anomalous API calls or lateral movement in the cloud. Expected Outcome: Students understand not just how to deploy resources, but how those resources are monitored using AI-driven security services in production cloud environments.

details.title: Quick recap of Linux essentials: file system, permissions, processes.
Focus on log files: auth logs, syslog, web server logs.
How logs are collected and shipped (high level) to centralized systems.
AI-awareness note: these logs are primary input to AI-based SIEM and anomaly detection tools used by DevSecOps and SOC teams.

topics.title: Bash Scripting / Python Scripting for Automation (6 h)

details.title: Writing simple scripts to:

details.text: Rotate logs, parse basic log entries, extract IPs/counts.
Schedule tasks (cron introduction).

details.title: Short exercise: script that counts failed login attempts or unusual IP hits from logs.

Link to AI: explain that automation scripts help prepare data pipelines, and in real organizations this kind of data stream is fed to AI/ML-based monitoring engines. Expected Outcome: Students gain practical skill in automation and see their scripts/log parsing as a pre-step towards AI-driven analytics (even if AI models are not built here).
Git workflows (feature branches, pull requests, code reviews).
Branching models (GitFlow, trunk-based development).
Emphasize secure practices: code review, commit signing basics.
CI concepts: automated build, test, and basic security checks.
Simple pipeline design with GitHub Actions / Jenkins / GitLab CI.
Example: pipeline that runs tests and a basic static analysis tool on each commit.

topics.title: AI-Driven Monitoring in CI/CD (2 h – conceptual)

details.title: How AI/ML is used in DevSecOps to:

details.text: Analyze code changes, build logs, deployment metrics, and flag anomalous or risky patterns.

details.title: Real-world examples:

details.text: Tools that use AI to detect unusual commit patterns or potential malicious commits before merge.
AI-driven monitoring tools that look at CI/CD logs to detect suspicious deployment behaviors or configuration drifts.

details.title: Place this as “what industry tools do behind the scenes” – no need to implement AI here, but students must understand that CI/CD pipelines are now augmented with AI-based security controls. Expected Outcome: Students understand CI/CD not only as automation, but as a data-rich pipeline where AI can continuously observe and improve security and reliability.
Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability (CIA).
Authentication, authorization, non-repudiation.
Basic threat modeling: assets, threats, vulnerabilities, risk.
Firewalls: packet filtering, stateful inspection.
IDS/IPS overview:

details.text: Signature-based vs anomaly-based detection.
Placement in network (edge, host-based).

details.title: Secure configuration concepts: hardening OS, disabling unused services.
How AI Intrusion Prevention Systems (AI‑IPS) work:

details.text: Use ML and behavior analytics to detect and prevent threats by analyzing patterns in traffic and logs.

details.title: Advantages over traditional signature-based tools: adaptability, fewer false positives (if designed well), ability to detect novel attacks.
Short case example:

details.text: AI‑based system monitoring internal East‑West traffic and automatically isolating compromised endpoints when anomalous lateral movement is detected.

details.title: Tie back to earlier: logs, flows, metrics students see are the inputs for these AI systems. Expected Outcome: Students can clearly articulate the difference between traditional IDS/IPS and AI-driven IDS/IPS, and see where AI fits into SecOps and cloud security operations.

semesters.description: (DevOps Advanced - 16 h + Cloud Architecture & MLOps Touchpoint – 10 h + Security Engineering Essentials – 12 h + Mini Project – 10 h)
pre_requisites: Students should be comfortable with Linux basics (shell, files, permissions), Git and simple
CI/CD pipelines, core cloud services on at least one provider (VM, storage, security groups/IAM
basics), container fundamentals (Docker), and have built/deployed at least one small DB‑backed
app, so this semester can focus on Infrastructure‑as‑Code, full DevSecOps pipelines,
cloud‑native architecture with containers/Kubernetes and MLOps touchpoints, and security

details.title: Overview of Terraform / CloudFormation / Ansible concepts.
Writing simple IaC definitions for:

details.text: A VM, security group, and storage.

details.title: Applying, updating, and destroying infrastructure via code.
Basic security best practices in IaC: least privilege IAM, avoiding hard‑coded secrets.
AI awareness in IaC (conceptual):

details.text: How AI-powered IaC review tools can scan Terraform/templates to detect risky patterns (public resources, overly broad IAM policies).

details.title: Designing a full pipeline (e.g., GitHub Actions/Jenkins/GitLab CI) for a sample app:

details.text: Stages: build → test → security checks → deploy.

details.title: Integrating basic security gates: SAST/DAST tool invocation in the pipeline.
AI-driven DevSecOps concept (2–3 h inside this block):

details.text: How AI/ML is increasingly embedded into CI/CD pipelines to: ○ Analyze build/test logs for anomalies and failure patterns. ○ Flag suspicious commits or dependency changes automatically.

Example discussion: AI agents suggesting fixes for security findings or blocking risky merges (code or config). Expected Outcome: Students can build a basic DevSecOps pipeline and understand how AI can be integrated into IaC review and CI/CD monitoring to strengthen security and efficiency.

details.title: Designing highly available systems: multi‑AZ/multi‑region basics.
Autoscaling and load balancing concepts:

details.text: Horizontal vs vertical scaling, health checks, failover.

details.title: Logging and monitoring basics (cloud metrics, alerts).
Container concepts (Docker images/containers).
Basic Kubernetes objects: Pod, Deployment, Service.
Deploying a simple containerized app to a cluster (demo/guided).
Where AI/ML workloads sit in cloud architectures:

details.text: Containerized ML services, model endpoints, batch vs real‑time inference.

details.title: Basics of MLOps:

details.text: Versioning models, deploying models using CI/CD, monitoring drift at a high level.

details.title: Security angle:

details.text: Concept that DevOps/Cloud engineers must help secure ML pipelines, including protecting data, models, and endpoints. Expected Outcome: Students can reason about cloud architectures that host both traditional and AI/ML workloads, and understand their role in securing and operating such systems.

details.title: Recap of Nmap; intro to Nessus/other scanners (demo level).
Running a basic scan on a lab target; interpreting severity, CVSS scores.
Exporting reports and prioritizing remediation.
New AI concept:

details.text: Explain how AI can assist in triaging and correlating vulnerability scan results with other signals (logs, config, exploit feeds) to prioritize what matters most.

details.title: Quick recap of OWASP Top 10, tying back to earlier semesters.
Emphasize secure coding patterns and configuration in cloud-native apps.
Brief view on API security in microservices architectures.

topics.title: AI-Driven Threat Detection & DevSecOps Practices – 2 h

details.title: Discuss AI-driven security controls that integrate into DevOps:

details.text: AI-based SAST/DAST augmentation (prioritizing findings, auto‑suggesting fixes).
AI‑driven anomaly detection in cloud runtime (containers & Kubernetes clusters).

details.title: High-level view of agentic AI in security (copilots for security engineers, AI assistance in runbooks/incident response). Expected Outcome: Students not only know traditional scanning and OWASP fundamentals, but also how AI enhances vulnerability management and runtime threat detection in modern DevSecOps setups.

topics.title: Teams design and implement an Infrastructure + Deployment project that must include:

details.title: IaC (Terraform/CloudFormation/Ansible or similar) for core infra.
A containerized application deployed via a CI/CD pipeline.
Basic security controls (restricted security groups, least‑privilege IAM, basic scanning in pipeline). AI-integrated options:
Integrate a step (even if simulated/demo) where:

details.text: The pipeline exports logs/artifacts that would be consumed by AI‑based analysis tools, or
A “dummy” AI check step is documented in the pipeline (students describe how, in industry, this would be an AI-based risk analysis or anomaly detection step).

topics.title: AI-Assisted IaC Review / Threat Detection Option

details.title: Document how IaC templates and config files would be passed to an AI‑powered IaC security tool (conceptual flow).
OR produce a short report discussing how AI could improve their project’s vulnerability triage, alert correlation, or incident response automation.
